speaker: interviewer
text: November the 7th, 1995, we're in the home of Hedy Epstein. The interviewer is Julie Heifetz in St. Louis, Missouri, USA. And it's all yours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Hedy, you were explaining your-- your reaction when your parents told you you were going on the children's transport, thinking perhaps you were adopted, and they wanted to get rid of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But, when I was on the train, my parents were on the platform, and the windows of the compartment were open. And we were still chit chatting, sort of small talk. You know, like, my parents were telling me to be sure to be polite when I get to England, to say please and thank you, which were some of the few English words that I knew. And they were trying to be cheerful, trying to smile. But as the train began to move out of the station, and I joined the transport, by the way, in Frankfurt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they ran to the end of the platform. And I could see the tears streaming down their face. And I knew, in that instant, that they really loved me. That this was a great sacrifice that they made because they loved me. That, in a way, they really gave me-- this is not what I was thinking then, but this act on their part really gave me life a second time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As the train was pulling out of the station, and they were running to the end of the platform, I was looking out of the window, and they were getting smaller and smaller. And finally, they were just two dots, and then, they were gone. What I did not know at that time is that this was the last time I saw my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you-- how do you understand that last conversation now with your father, about the immortality of the June bug?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I understand that he was very uncomfortable talking to me about sex education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you think that's still what he was saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm pretty sure that's what it was. Because you know, what does immortality, what is having-- you know, I mean, and the way he questioned me. You know, why is it-- why are they immortal? I mean, he was steering me in that direction, but was uncomfortable to tell me. And you know, and the-- my final instructions were-- you know, when-- don't go with-- I mean, both my parents said this, and not during that hike that I took with my father, but subsequently. You know, if-- don't talk to strangers. Don't go with strangers. And it wasn't necessarily stressed strange-- strange men, but just strangers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think I-- you know, when I think back, I think I know what they were trying to tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you got on this train with all of these other children, can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember much about who I shared the compartment with or what happened on this trip. But I know I immediately-- after I no longer could see my parents, I immediately got out some paper, and I started to write to my parents and to tell them what I was really feeling. And I'm glad that I was able to do this and that-- and I know they received those letters, because I wrote several. When the train stopped the first time in Cologne, I handed the letter to someone and asked them to please mail it. And then it stopped again shortly before we crossed the border into Holland, and I, by that time, had another letter. And I-- my parents responded and told me that they had received those letters. I'm glad I had the opportunity to tell them how much I understood that they loved me, and how much I loved them, and how much I knew that it was a great sacrifice that they had performed in letting me go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to-- one of the-- as soon as the train crossed the border into Holland, it stopped somewhere. And there were some ladies on the platform who gave us cookies and juice. And I still remember this, how good those cookies were. At 6 o'clock that evening, we boarded the ship in Hook Van Holland. That's H-O-O-K, new word V-A-N, new word H-O-L-L-A-N-D. And the boat was not to leave until midnight and arrive in Harwich, H-A-R-W-I-C-H, England the following morning at 6 o'clock. I don't really remember much about boarding the ship or who I-- I know I shared [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the room, or whatever, the cabin, with someone. I have no idea anymore who that was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What I do remember is I, at some point, needed to go to the bathroom. And I had learned that bathroom in English was water closet, or WC, and so I looked. I walked around the ship, and it was a small ship. I walked around, looking for either water closet or WC, and I couldn't find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I decided, well, I'm just going to look on one side. And then, when I come the other, when I come back, I'll look on the other side. Maybe I missed it and I did not see it. And so I asked one of the personnel from the boat, where is the water closet? And he said, there is none. And I said, where is the WC? There is none.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I began to panic. I mean, I didn't need to go to the bathroom urgently at that point, but until 6 o'clock tomorrow morning, before we get to England, I don't think I can make it. And at midnight, when the motors were all revved up, and the boat was ready to leave, though, we were told we should not leave our cabin, I went up on deck, and I think there was a little waterfall up there. [LAUGHS] And the following morning, I discovered, and it said, men and women. But I was so single-minded on water closet or WC, that even though I might have seen that, it didn't register. And I knew what those two words meant. In Harwich, we boarded a train to London, to London's Liverpool Street Station. And there, we were taken to a huge hall. And there were people there waiting for to receive those of us who had arrived. Some of the children went into private homes or foster homes. This went into institutional settings. I went into a private home, a Jewish home. The way that I was able to go to England is my father, in his research looking for people who could sponsor our exit from Germany, found a cousin of my grandfather's, my mother's father's cousin, a woman by the name of Anna, A-N-N-A, Simon, S-I-M-O-N. She was in her 70s at the time, and she had come to England when she was about 18 months old. And she agreed to sponsor me but was not willing to sponsor my parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I guess it would have meant too much of a financial responsibility for her. And she did not want me to stay at her home. She was still working in her own business at that time, and actually had very little contact with me. Most of it was with her-- it was through her daughter, Beatrice Mayer, B-E-A-T-R-I-C-E. Mayer is M-A-Y-E-R. And Beatrice Mayer had contacted Rabbi Amias, A-M-I-A-S, who in turn found the family that I stayed with. And the family was-- name was-- the husband's name was Sydney Rose, R-O-S-E. They lived in Edgeware, E-D-G-E-W-A-R-E, which is sort of a suburb of London. They had three children. One of them-- three daughters, one was older than I. And the middle child was the same age as I. And then, there was a younger one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I arrived in England on the 19th of May, 1939. I just recently attended a conference, a children's transport conference. Maybe later on, I can talk a little bit about that, because that's where I learned some of how this all came about. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Two or three days after I arrived in England, I started school in Edgeware Senior High School. And I had absolutely no idea what was going on at school because my English was so limited, and the Rose family spoke no German. And so I was forced to learn English because how else was I going to communicate. I stayed in written contact with my parents. We-- I received letters from them almost daily. And I wrote almost daily to them. Life at the Rose family was not easy for me, but I never shared that with my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was told, or was given to understand, and I understood it in more ways than one very quickly, that when I lived in Germany, I was starving, which was really not true. Because the only thing that I remember is that butter was rationed. And you certainly can live well and eat well without ever touching butter. And I was told now that I see that food is plentiful, I will want everything, and that's not healthy for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I need to increase my food intake gradually. And I stayed with the Rose family for 10 weeks. And the entire 10 weeks, my diet consisted of a piece of toast, one piece of toast, and butter, and a cup of tea for breakfast, two pieces of toast and a cup of tea for lunch, and two more pieces of toast and tea for dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: On Sunday mornings, in addition to my one piece of toast and a cup of tea for breakfast, I also got a cookie. That was perhaps because Mr. Rose, on Sunday mornings, served tea and cookies to everyone in the family. And I shared a room with the middle daughter. And since he brought her tea and a cookie, I guess he felt he ought to give me tea and a cookie also. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But two weeks before school was out, and school was out the end of July, I got a phone call from Beatrice Mayer, who had left with her family and her mother for France two days after I arrived in England, where they normally spent their summer. But because it looked like a war might break out, they came back earlier. And so she called me to find out how I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was about 4:30 in the afternoon. And she said, how are you? And for the first time, I was honest. And I said, hungry. And she said, well, when are you going to eat dinner? And I said, well, it won't make any difference. And so, she said, well, why-- and she sensed that something was wrong. And so she said, well, why didn't you come and visit us on Saturday, and then we can talk? And I said, I can't. And she said, why can't you come? What are you doing? And I said, nothing, but I don't have any money, and I need to take the subway to come to your house. And so she said, don't you get an allowance? First of all, I didn't even know what the word meant. But I-- and then she explained. And I said, no, I don't get any money. And I had been allowed to take the equivalent of 10 German marks out of Germany, and I had not touched it. I was saving that for when things get really, really bad. And I didn't think they were that bad yet. And so, Beatrice Mayer said, why don't you have Mrs. Rose come to the phone?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they talked for a while, and then I was put back on the phone. And she said, well, Mrs. Rose will give you car fare, and you come to our house on Saturday, and then we can talk. And we not only talked, but I ate, and I ate, and I ate as long as I was there. And Miss Mayer was saying to me, you know, I don't mind you eating. But you know, you're going to get sick. And I said, I don't care. But I'm going to eat. I'm going to eat ahead for. And so, she told me, she-- you know, school is going to be out in another two weeks or so. So, why don't you stay at the-- with the Rose family, and we'll try to find another place for you. It may not be in the same school area. And here's some money, and buy yourself something to eat on the way to school and on your way home. But don't say anything about that to the Rose family. And don't say anything about leaving, I will handle all of that. And I did buy myself something every day on my way to school and on my way back home. I bought myself Cadbury's chocolate with hazelnuts. I remember one of the children had brought that to school and offered me some. And other children had offered me things that they had brought to school. But I never took any of it, because there was nothing that I could bring. I mean, I could bring maybe a piece of toast. And I remember smelling that Cadbury chocolate that this one child had. And I had found the store. And I used to stand in front of the window with my nose probably right up to the window, looking at these candy bars. But now, I had the money to buy them. And that's how I supplemented my diet. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I then was placed with another family, the Harry Simmons family. That's S-I-M-M-O-N-S. They also lived in Edgeware. The Rose family were probably very much middle class. The Simmons family was quite a poor family. They were Jewish also. And they had a son, David, who was a year younger than I, and a little girl, Janice, who was about six at the time that I came there. And they were told of what my experience was with the Rose family. And so they practically force fed me to where I had to say, I can't eat anymore. And I think they probably would have deprived themselves of food, just to make sure that I would not be hungry. I stayed with them until just before my 16th birthday, in August of 1940. Mrs. Simon, who had been paying both the Rose family and the Simmons family for my board and lodging, told me that in England, you only have to go to school until you're 16, and you're going to be 16 in August, so you're going to have to drop out of school, and you're going to have to find a job, because I will no longer financially support you. So, if you want a roof over your head and food in your stomach, you're going to have to find a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was just absolutely petrified. Because, first of all, I had not finished my education. And job training, I haven't had. And how does one look for a job? I had no idea. And I expressed my concerns to Mrs. Simon's daughter, Beatrice Mayer, and she found a job for me with the cantor, Cantor Tachlicky And I am not sure of the spelling anymore. I'll try my best, T-A-C-H-L-I-C-K-Y.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cantor Tachlicky was either divorced or separated from his wife, and he had a 14-year-old daughter, who lived with him. There was also another man living in the house, who was a refugee from Germany. And I was to be the companion to his 14-year-old daughter. And my job, essentially, was to take her to school and pick her up from school, be there when she comes home from school, play with her, talk to her, do some dusting around the house. And for that, I had my board and lodging, and I got five shillings a week. And I was quite sure I was well on my way to being a millionaire now.

speaker: interviewer
text: During all of this time, the communication from your parents was from what address, Hedy?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents and I communicated directly with each other. My parents were still in Kippenheim until England declared war on Germany on September 3rd, 1939. Then, it was no longer possible to communicate directly. And we used two avenues to stay in touch with each other. One of them was through the Red Cross. You could send messages containing not more than 25 words. And the other was we knew some people in Switzerland and Holland, both of them were neutral countries. And my parents would write a letter and send it to our friends in Switzerland or in Holland, and they would take it out of the envelope, and readdress a new envelope, and put my parents' letter in there, and forward it to me. And then, I would use the same route to communicate with my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After, when I moved from the Rose family to the Simmons family, that was still before the war broke out. And of course, my parents wanted to know why I had changed an address. If all was so well, why did I move? And so I had to tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents were very perturbed that I had not honestly told them what had happened. And there was a question of trust that was involved there. But I had not communicated it to them because I felt they have enough problems in Germany, and why should I share my problems with them, especially when I felt they couldn't do anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you know of the problems in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, you know, I-- especially, you know, once England had declared war on Germany, and I-- I felt that my parents probably were holding back on sharing with me some of the things that had happened. Like something that I found out many years later is, for instance, my parents were moved-- had to move out of the house that we lived in, and they had to move into the same house where my grandmother and my aunt and uncle lived. And I guess, Jews had to move closer together, not in a ghetto, but they just had to-- had less were afforded less living space. I understand my father was sent to work for a farmer in a neighboring community. My parents never shared that with me. These are things that I found out afterwards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we remained in touch with each other, but really not-- and we had to be careful. You know, I couldn't say something or other about London, because the letter was not supposedly coming from England, it was coming from Holland or from Switzerland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, I received a letter from my parents notifying me that, as my father put it, we have recently changed our address. Actually, what happened was on October 22, 1940, all the Jews from that section of Germany where I come from, which is Baden, B-A-D-E-N, and another section of Germany called Wurttemberg, W-U umlaut -R-T-T-E-M-B-E-R-G were deported to a camp in France called Camp De Gurs. That's C-A-M-P, new word D-E, new word G-U-R-S. Camp De Gurs is located in the foothills of the Pyrenean Mountains, in an area that was actually Vichy, France. France had already been occupied by the Germans in the spring of 1940. But that one section of France, which was referred to as Vichy, France, sort of seceded from the rest of France and collaborated with the Nazis. And that's where Camp De Gurs and several other camps were located. It was perhaps an aberration of the war, but my parents were able to communicate directly with me once they were in Camp De Gurs. It went through the censorship. And they were limited as to how often and how much they could write. Men and women were separate in Camp De Gurs, separated by barbed wire. I understand that in the beginning, once a month, men and women were allowed to spend one hour together. In fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned that afterwards. Again, my parents didn't share that with me. But I have in my possession a photograph, a family photograph that was taken in Gurs. It had to be taken fairly early. I don't know who took it. I don't know how it got out. And it shows men and women, my family, together. And it had to be taken fairly early, because they look, still, quite well. When I was in the Holocaust Museum in Washington DC, I saw some photographs in their archives of people taken later on in the camp, and they looked like mere skeletons. So, that's why I'm thinking it was taken early. My grandfather, my mother's father, is not on that photograph. I know that he died two months after he arrived there. Now, whether he was still alive, or maybe he was too sick to be on that photograph, I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But my parents never ever told me of the horrendous conditions in that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the spring of 1941, my father was sent to another camp called Camp Les Milles. That's C-A-M-P, new word L-E-S, and new word, capital M-I-L-E-S-- M-I-L-L-E-S, which is located just north of Marseilles, France. The camp actually was a former brick factory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And because they were limited as to how often they could write and how much they could write, one page per person per week, in order to stay in touch with each other-- that is my mother and father to stay in touch with each other and also with me, my father would write one page and then send it to my mother, and she would write on the reverse page and then forward it to me. And the following week, my mother would write one page, send it to my father. He write on the reverse page, send it to me. And I could write as often and as much as I wanted to. At one point, it was possible to send money to my parents. And I understand there was a canteen there where you could supplement your diet. Food was extremely expensive, plus the Nazis skimmed off quite a bit at the-- from the amount that was sent. And I didn't really have any money. I was working, but I was just barely scratching out of a living. And I had a stamp collection that I had taken out of Germany illegally. And I sold that stamp collection and used that money to send to my parents. I tried to find other sources where I could maybe get some money to send to my parents and wasn't really successful in doing so. I had lost contact with Mrs. Simon and her daughter, because they apparently left London once the air raids started, and I didn't know where they were. And I wrote to the old address, and I don't know if it was forwarded. I just, I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, you know, my father did not tell me of the conditions in that camp. There were-- it's perhaps a form of resistance. And you know, normally, we think of resistance that you pick up a weapon, and you fight, you go up on the barricades. But there's another way of resisting, and I think both my parents resisted passively in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
